Airline Route Profitability

2026-04-19

Introduction

Airlines operate in one of the most cost-intensive industries in the world, so understanding what makes a flight profitable is important for route planning, fleet management, and revenue strategy. This project analyzes a simulated one-year dataset of flight-level financial data for a major Middle Eastern airline operating from Dubai (DXB). Because Dubai is a global hub connecting regional, medium-distance, and intercontinental destinations, it is a strong setting for studying how different route types influence profitability.

The goal of this analysis is to answer a business question: What factors are most strongly associated with airline profitability? In particular, this report investigates whether profitability is more strongly affected by route category, season, demand level, or load factor.

To answer this question, the report uses data cleaning, grouped summaries, tables, and visualizations. The analysis focuses on key variables such as Profit, Profit_Margin, Load_Factor, Season, Demand_Level, and Route_Category. The final goal is not only to identify which flights make more money, but also to understand which flights are more efficient and why.

Packages Required

# tidyverse: data import, wrangling, and plotting
library(tidyverse)

# tidyr: reshaping data into tidy format
library(tidyr)

# lubridate: converting and working with dates
library(lubridate)

# knitr: making clean summary tables
library(knitr)

# scales: formatting labels as dollars and percentages
library(scales)

Data Preparation

Data Source

The dataset used in this project is the Airline Route Profitability Dataset from Kaggle by Muhammad Waleed:

Airline Route Profitability Dataset

This dataset simulates a full year of flight-level financial data for a major Middle Eastern carrier based in Dubai (DXB). Each row represents a single flight, and the data includes route category, season, demand, aircraft type, passenger load, revenue, costs, and profit.

The airline operates across three route categories:

  • Short-Haul (1 to 3.5 hours): DOH, MCT, KWI, BAH, RUH, JED, CAI, AMM
  • Medium-Haul (2.5 to 7 hours): BOM, DEL, IST, LHR, BLR, HYD, MAA, KHI, LHE, CMB
  • Long-Haul (6.5 to 16.5 hours): JFK, SYD, MEL, SIN, HKG, BKK, KUL, CDG, FRA, LAX, SFO, ORD

Load and Clean the Data

airline_data <- read_csv("airline_route_profitability.csv")
# Replace missing numeric values with the median
airline_clean <- airline_data %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric),
                ~ ifelse(is.na(.), median(., na.rm = TRUE), .)))

# Replace missing text values with "Unknown"
airline_clean <- airline_clean %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character),
                ~ ifelse(is.na(.), "Unknown", .))) %>%
  mutate(Flight_Date = ymd(Flight_Date))

Check Missing Values After Cleaning

missing_after <- airline_clean %>%
  summarize(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "Variable",
               values_to = "Missing_After") %>%
  filter(Missing_After > 0)

kable(missing_after, caption = "Remaining Missing Values After Cleaning")
Remaining Missing Values After Cleaning
Variable Missing_After

Final Dataset Size

dataset_shape <- tibble(
  Rows = nrow(airline_clean),
  Columns = ncol(airline_clean)
)

kable(dataset_shape, caption = "Final Dataset Size")
Final Dataset Size
Rows Columns
7974 33

Summary of Key Variables

key_variables <- tibble(
  Variable = c("Profit", "Profit_Margin", "Load_Factor", "Route_Category", "Season", "Demand_Level"),
  Description = c(
    "Net earnings per flight after total costs",
    "Profit as a percentage of total revenue",
    "Proportion of seats filled on a flight",
    "Short-, medium-, or long-haul route grouping",
    "Season category assigned in the dataset",
    "Demand intensity assigned to each flight"
  ),
  Why_Important = c(
    "Main outcome variable",
    "Measures efficiency instead of just scale",
    "Measures how full the aircraft was",
    "Represents route scale and operating pattern",
    "Captures seasonal demand timing",
    "Captures customer demand pressure"
  )
)

kable(key_variables, caption = "Key Variables Used in the Analysis")
Key Variables Used in the Analysis
Variable Description Why_Important
Profit Net earnings per flight after total costs Main outcome variable
Profit_Margin Profit as a percentage of total revenue Measures efficiency instead of just scale
Load_Factor Proportion of seats filled on a flight Measures how full the aircraft was
Route_Category Short-, medium-, or long-haul route grouping Represents route scale and operating pattern
Season Season category assigned in the dataset Captures seasonal demand timing
Demand_Level Demand intensity assigned to each flight Captures customer demand pressure

Correct Season Definitions from the Actual Dataset

After checking the actual CSV, the season labels in the dataset correspond to the following month groupings:

  • Peak: January to February and December
  • Shoulder: March to April and October to November
  • Normal: May and September
  • Low: June to August

This correction is important because it changes the interpretation of the seasonal and monthly profit results.

Exploratory Data Analysis

Profit Summary by Season

profit_by_season <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    Flight_Count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Avg_Profit))

kable(profit_by_season, digits = 2, caption = "Average Profit and Margin by Season")
Average Profit and Margin by Season
Season Avg_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin Flight_Count
Peak 110278.75 17.55 2002
Shoulder 82675.09 10.14 2646
Normal 63480.49 3.33 1325
Low 25903.39 -8.50 2001

With the corrected season definitions, the pattern is clear and internally consistent: Peak performs best, followed by Shoulder, then Normal, while Low performs worst.

Profit Summary by Route Category

profit_by_route <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    Flight_Count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Avg_Profit))

kable(profit_by_route, digits = 2, caption = "Average Profit and Margin by Route Category")
Average Profit and Margin by Route Category
Route_Category Avg_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin Flight_Count
Long Haul 160410.04 22.71 3225
Medium Haul 30232.15 12.96 2563
Short Haul -8841.14 -26.12 2186

Long-haul routes earn the most total profit, while short-haul routes are the weakest overall. However, margins show that earning more total dollars does not always mean being proportionally more efficient.

Profit Summary by Demand Level

profit_by_demand <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    Flight_Count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Avg_Profit))

kable(profit_by_demand, digits = 2, caption = "Average Profit and Margin by Demand Level")
Average Profit and Margin by Demand Level
Demand_Level Avg_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin Flight_Count
High 107463.74 17.69 4630
Medium 23302.49 -9.73 3344

Demand level shows one of the strongest patterns in the dataset. High-demand flights consistently outperform medium-demand flights in both total profit and profit margin.

Research Questions

1. How does profit spread across all flights?

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Profit)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 50000, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Profit Across Flights",
    x = "Profit (USD)",
    y = "Number of Flights"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Most flights earn between roughly $0 and $500K, while a smaller number earn much more. A few flights also operate at a loss.

2. Are long-haul routes more profitable than short-haul routes?

route_summary <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    Flight_Count = n(),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_Profit = median(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(route_summary, digits = 2, caption = "Profit Summary by Route Category")
Profit Summary by Route Category
Route_Category Flight_Count Avg_Profit Median_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin
Long Haul 3225 160410.04 145271.11 22.71
Medium Haul 2563 30232.15 28196.63 12.96
Short Haul 2186 -8841.14 -5710.52 -26.12 
ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Route_Category, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Profit by Route Category",
    x = "Route Category",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Route_Category, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Profit Margin by Route Category",
    x = "Route Category",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Long-haul flights earn the highest total profit, but the margin gap is smaller than the raw dollar gap. This means long-haul routes are larger in scale, but not automatically the most efficient.

3. Does season affect profitability?

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Profit by Season",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Season, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Profit Margin by Season",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

With the corrected definitions, season clearly affects profitability. Peak season has the strongest performance, while Low season has the weakest.

4. How does demand level influence profitability?

demand_summary <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    Flight_Count = n(),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_Profit = median(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    Median_Profit_Margin = median(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(demand_summary, digits = 2, caption = "Profit Summary by Demand Level")
Profit Summary by Demand Level
Demand_Level Flight_Count Avg_Profit Median_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin Median_Profit_Margin
High 4630 107463.74 43018.31 17.69 21.91
Medium 3344 23302.49 2530.11 -9.73 1.67 
ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Demand_Level, y = Profit)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Profit by Demand Level",
    x = "Demand Level",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Demand_Level, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Profit Margin by Demand Level",
    x = "Demand Level",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Demand level remains the strongest overall driver. It produces a cleaner separation than season, especially in profit margin.

5. Do fuller flights lead to higher profit?

ggplot(airline_clean, aes(x = Load_Factor, y = Profit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Load Factor vs Profit",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line marks break-even profit",
    x = "Load Factor",
    y = "Profit (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

All flights in this dataset already have a high load factor, so the data looks compact and clustered. Even then, a lot of flights still fall below the break-even line. This tells us that load factor alone does not guarantee profitability. Other fixed costs like fuel, crew salaries, and airport fees also play a significant role.

6. Is profit more influenced by season or by demand level?

season_compare <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(Group_Type = "Season",
         Group = Season) %>%
  select(Group_Type, Group, Avg_Profit, Avg_Profit_Margin)

demand_compare <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Demand_Level) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(Group_Type = "Demand Level",
         Group = Demand_Level) %>%
  select(Group_Type, Group, Avg_Profit, Avg_Profit_Margin)

comparison_table <- bind_rows(season_compare, demand_compare)

kable(comparison_table, digits = 2, caption = "Comparing Season and Demand Level Effects")
Comparing Season and Demand Level Effects
Group_Type Group Avg_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin
Season Low 25903.39 -8.50
Season Normal 63480.49 3.33
Season Peak 110278.75 17.55
Season Shoulder 82675.09 10.14
Demand Level High 107463.74 17.69
Demand Level Medium 23302.49 -9.73

Season matters much more than it seemed before the correction, but demand level still shows the cleanest overall profitability split.

7. Which season and route combinations are most profitable?

combo_summary <- airline_clean %>%
  group_by(Season, Route_Category) %>%
  summarize(
    Flight_Count = n(),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Profit_Margin = mean(Profit_Margin, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Avg_Profit))

kable(combo_summary, digits = 2, caption = "Profitability by Season and Route Category")
Profitability by Season and Route Category
Season Route_Category Flight_Count Avg_Profit Avg_Profit_Margin
Peak Long Haul 812 230395.04 31.54
Shoulder Long Haul 1068 181075.91 26.02
Normal Long Haul 533 149345.35 22.06
Low Long Haul 812 70506.73 9.95
Peak Medium Haul 643 55383.42 23.74
Shoulder Medium Haul 849 36260.97 16.67
Normal Medium Haul 424 20026.77 9.44
Low Medium Haul 647 4013.20 -0.32
Peak Short Haul 547 -3499.55 -10.51
Shoulder Short Haul 729 -7429.86 -20.74
Normal Short Haul 368 -10817.31 -30.84
Low Short Haul 542 -14788.44 -45.90

The strongest combination is Peak + Long Haul, while Low + Short Haul is the weakest.

8. How does profit change month by month?

monthly_profit <- airline_clean %>%
  mutate(Month = floor_date(Flight_Date, unit = "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarize(
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(monthly_profit, aes(x = Month, y = Avg_Profit)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "navy", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(scale = 1e-3, suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Monthly Profit Over Time",
    subtitle = "Red dashed line marks break-even profit",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Average Profit (USD)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The monthly trend is consistent with the dataset’s season definitions. Winter months perform best because they fall into Peak season, while summer months perform worst because they fall into Low season.

Conclusion

This report studied the profitability of airline routes for a simulated Dubai-based airline using a full year of flight-level financial data. The goal was to identify whether route category, season, demand level, or load factor had the strongest relationship with profit.

Several clear findings emerged:

  • Demand level is the strongest predictor of profitability. High-demand flights consistently outperform medium-demand flights in both profit and profit margin.
  • Long-haul routes earn the most total profit. However, their profit-margin advantage is smaller than their raw profit advantage.
  • Season clearly matters once the dataset’s real season definitions are used. Peak season corresponds to January, February, and December, while Low season corresponds to June through August.
  • Load factor helps, but is not enough on its own. Full flights can still lose money if operating costs are too high.
  • The strongest combination is Peak + Long Haul.

Limitations

This dataset is simulated rather than taken directly from a real airline, so the findings should be interpreted as realistic modeled patterns rather than exact operational truth. In addition, the data reflects one Dubai-based airline setting, so the results may not apply equally to every airline business model.

Future Improvements

Future work could explore profitability by aircraft type, by specific destination, or through predictive modeling using demand, season, route category, and load factor.
